Introduce \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,\text{base_A}\) and
\(\tilde{M}(t) = \tilde{A}(t)^{-1} Q\, \tilde{A}(t)^{-1}\). With \(Q = I\),
\(\text{base_A} = I\), so \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,I\) and \(\tilde{M}(t) = \tilde{A}(t)^{-2}\).
Write \(A(t) = \tilde{A}(t)\), \(M(t) = \tilde{M}(t)\).

With \(B_0(t) = t\,\text{base_B0} + P\mu\), the tangency point satisfies
\[
\beta_j(t) \;=\; A(t)^{-1}\bigl(\bar{c}_j - B_0(t)\bigr)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
A(t)\,\beta_j(t) \;=\; \bar{c}_j - B_0(t).
\]
Let \(\hat{\beta} = \arg\min_\beta RSS(\beta)\) be the ML estimator. Define the **residual**
\[
r_j(t) \;:=\; A(t)\,\bigl(\beta_j(t) - \hat{\beta}\bigr),
\]
so that \(\beta_j(t) - \hat{\beta} = A(t)^{-1}\,r_j(t)\).

**Linear form of \(r_j(t)\).** Using \(r_j(t) = A(t)\bigl(\beta_j(t) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)\),
\(A(t)\,\beta_j(t) = \bar{c}_j - B_0(t)\), \(B_0(t) = t\,\text{base_B0} + P\mu\), and
\(A(t) = P + t\,\text{base_A}\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
r_j(t)
&\;=\; A(t)\,\bigl(\beta_j(t) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(A(t)\,\beta_j(t)\bigr) \;-\; \bigl(A(t)\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - B_0(t)\bigr) \;-\; \bigl(A(t)\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - (t\,\text{base_B0} + P\mu)\bigr) \;-\; \bigl((P + t\,\text{base_A})\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\bigr) \;+\; t\,\bigl(-\text{base_B0} - \text{base_A}\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\bigr) \;+\; t\,\bigl(-\bigl(X^{T} W\,(\alpha - y) + (X^{T} W\, X)\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\bigr) \;+\; t\,\bigl(-\bigl(X^{T} W\,(\alpha - y) + (X^{T} W\, X)\,(X^{T} W\, X)^{-1} X^{T} W\,(y - \alpha)\bigr)\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\bigr) \;+\; t\,\bigl(-\bigl(X^{T} W\,(\alpha - y) + X^{T} W\,(y - \alpha)\bigr)\bigr)
\\
&\;=\; \bigl(\bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\bigr) \;+\; t\cdot 0
\\
&\;=\; \bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}.
\end{aligned}
\]
(Substitute \(\text{base_B0} = X^{T} W\,(\alpha - y)\), \(\text{base_A} = X^{T} W\, X\), and
\(\hat{\beta} = (X^{T} W\, X)^{-1} X^{T} W\,(y - \alpha)\); then
\((X^{T} W\, X)\,\hat{\beta} = X^{T} W\,(y - \alpha)\), so the bracketed sum cancels.)
With \(a_j = -\bigl(\text{base_B0} + \text{base_A}\,\hat{\beta}\bigr)\) and
\(b_j = \bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\), we have \(a_j = 0\) (as shown) and
\(r_j(t) = a_j\,t + b_j = b_j\).

## 3. Lower bounding the quadratic form

The quadratic form is
\[
\|\beta_j(t) - \hat{\beta}\|_2^2 \;=\; r_j(t)^{T}\, M(t)\, r_j(t) \;=\; r_j(t)^{T}\, \tilde{M}(t)\, r_j(t),
\]
where \(r_j(t)\) is the residual.
Decompose
\[
\tilde{M}(t) \;=\; \tilde{M}_0(t) \;+\; \bigl(\tilde{M}(t) - \tilde{M}_0(t)\bigr),
\]
where \(\tilde{M}_0(t) = \tilde{M}(d,0)\) at \(t = 1/d\). With \(Q = I\),
\(\tilde{A}_0(t) = t\,I\), so \(\tilde{M}_0(t) = (1/t^2)\,I\). For the residual \(r_j(t)\),
\[
r_j(t)^{T} M(t)\, r_j(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{t^2}\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2 \;+\; r_j(t)^{T}\bigl[\tilde{M}(t) - \tilde{M}_0(t)\bigr] r_j(t).
\]





## 4. Conjecture and proof (second term, \(Q = I\))

**Conjecture.** Define \(D(t) = \tilde{M}(t) - \tilde{M}_0(t)\). For \(t \ge t_{\mathrm{low}} = 1/\mathrm{upp}\),
\[
r_j^{T} D(t)\, r_j \;\ge\; r_j^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, r_j
\quad\text{for all } r_j,
\]
i.e. \(D(t) \succeq D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\) (Loewner order), so \(D(t)\) is increasing in \(t\).

**Theorem (case \(Q = I\)).** When \(Q = I\) and \(P \succ 0\), the conjecture holds.

*Proof.* With \(Q = I\), \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,I\) and
\(B(t) = Q^{1/2}\,\tilde{A}(t)^{-1}\,Q^{1/2} = (P + t\,I)^{-1}\).
Then \(\frac{dD}{dt} = 2\,\bigl(I/t^3 - B(t)^3\bigr)\). The matrix \(B(t)\) shares eigenvectors with \(P\).
If \(\lambda_i(P) > 0\) is an eigenvalue of \(P\), then \(B(t)\) has eigenvalue \(1/(\lambda_i(P) + t)\),
so \(B(t)^3\) has eigenvalue \(1/(\lambda_i(P) + t)^3\), and \(I/t^3 - B(t)^3\) has eigenvalues
\[
\mu_i(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{t^3} \;-\; \frac{1}{(\lambda_i(P) + t)^3}.
\]
For \(\frac{dD}{dt} \succeq 0\) we need \(\mu_i(t) \ge 0\) for all \(i\), i.e.
\((\lambda_i(P) + t)^3 \ge t^3\), hence \(\lambda_i(P) + t \ge t\), i.e. \(\lambda_i(P) \ge 0\).
Since \(P \succ 0\), all \(\lambda_i(P) > 0\), so \(\mu_i(t) > 0\). Thus \(\frac{dD}{dt} \succ 0\), so \(D(t)\) is increasing in \(t\). Hence
\(D(t) \succeq D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\) for \(t \ge t_{\mathrm{low}}\).

## 5. Lower bound using the conjecture

From section 3,
\[
r_j(t)^{T} M(t)\, r_j(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{t^2}\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2 \;+\; r_j(t)^{T}\bigl[\tilde{M}(t) - \tilde{M}_0(t)\bigr] r_j(t).
\]
By the conjecture, \(D(t) = \tilde{M}(t) - \tilde{M}_0(t) \succeq D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\) for \(t \ge t_{\mathrm{low}}\),
so \(r_j(t)^{T} D(t)\, r_j(t) \ge r_j(t)^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, r_j(t)\). Hence
\[
r_j(t)^{T} M(t)\, r_j(t) \;\ge\; \frac{1}{t^2}\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2 \;+\; r_j(t)^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, r_j(t).
\]

## 6. Minimization of the improved lower bound

Minimize over \(t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) the bound from section 5:
\[
f_j(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{t^2}\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2 \;+\; r_j(t)^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, r_j(t).
\]
Using \(r_j(t) = a_j\,t + b_j\) with \(a_j\), \(b_j\) from section 2, write
\(\|r_j(t)\|_2^2 = \alpha\,t^2 + \beta\,t + \gamma\)
where \(\alpha = \|a_j\|_2^2\), \(\beta = 2\,a_j^{T}b_j\), \(\gamma = \|b_j\|_2^2\). Also
\[
r_j(t)^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, r_j(t)
\;=\; \delta\,t^2 \;+\; \varepsilon\,t \;+\; \zeta,
\]
with \(\delta = a_j^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, a_j\),
\(\varepsilon = 2\,a_j^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, b_j\),
\(\zeta = b_j^{T} D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\, b_j\). Hence
\[
f_j(t) \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,t^2 + \beta\,t + \gamma}{t^2} \;+\; \delta\,t^2 + \varepsilon\,t + \zeta
\;=\; \alpha \;+\; \frac{\beta}{t} \;+\; \frac{\gamma}{t^2} \;+\; \delta\,t^2 \;+\; \varepsilon\,t \;+\; \zeta.
\]
Differentiate:
\[
f_j'(t) \;=\; -\frac{\beta}{t^2} \;-\; \frac{2\gamma}{t^3} \;+\; 2\delta\,t \;+\; \varepsilon.
\]
Setting \(f_j'(t) = 0\) and multiplying by \(t^3\) gives
\[
2\delta\,t^4 \;+\; \varepsilon\,t^3 \;-\; \beta\,t \;-\; 2\gamma \;=\; 0.
\]

**Formulas for roots.** For weak priors (\(P\) small), \(D(t_{\mathrm{low}})\) is near zero, so \(\delta\), \(\varepsilon\), \(\zeta\) are small. The \(\delta\,t^2 + \varepsilon\,t + \zeta\) term may still shift the minimizer.

- **Case \(\delta = \varepsilon = 0\):** The quartic reduces to \(-\beta\,t - 2\gamma = 0\), giving
  \[
  t^* \;=\; -\frac{2\gamma}{\beta} \;=\; -\frac{\|b_j\|_2^2}{a_j^{T}b_j}
  \quad\text{(when } a_j^{T}b_j \ne 0\text{)}.
  \]
  This is the minimizer of \((1/t^2)\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2\) alone.

- **Case \(\delta = 0\), \(\varepsilon \ne 0\):** The equation becomes the cubic
  \(\varepsilon\,t^3 - \beta\,t - 2\gamma = 0\). The closed-form cubic root(s) can be used; the relevant real root in \([t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) is the candidate.

- **General case (Ferrari's formula):** Write the quartic in monic form
  \(t^4 + a_3 t^3 + a_2 t^2 + a_1 t + a_0 = 0\) with
  \(a_3 = \varepsilon/(2\delta)\), \(a_2 = 0\), \(a_1 = -\beta/(2\delta)\), \(a_0 = -\gamma/\delta\).
  The resolvent cubic is \(y^3 + A\,y + B = 0\) with
  \[
  A \;=\; \frac{-\beta\varepsilon + 16\gamma\delta}{4\delta^2},
  \qquad
  B \;=\; -\frac{\beta^2\delta - \gamma\varepsilon^2}{4\delta^3}.
  \]
  By Cardano's formula, a real root is
  \(y_1 = \sqrt[3]{-B/2 + \sqrt{B^2/4 + A^3/27}}
  + \sqrt[3]{-B/2 - \sqrt{B^2/4 + A^3/27}}\) (take real cube roots). Define
  \[
  p \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{2\delta} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon^2}{4\delta^2} + 4y_1},
  \qquad
  q \;=\; y_1 \;\mp\; \sqrt{y_1^2 + \frac{4\gamma}{\delta}},
  \]
  where the \(\pm\) and \(\mp\) are chosen in the two pairs \((+,\,-)\) and \((-,\,+)\) to yield
  four combinations. The four roots are
  \[
  t \;=\; \frac{-p \;\pm\; \sqrt{p^2 - 8q}}{4},
  \]
  with both choices of \((\pm,\mp)\) in \((p,q)\) and both choices of \(\pm\) in the numerator.
  The minimizer is the real root in \([t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) that minimizes \(f_j(t)\), or an
  endpoint. For weak priors, \(t^* = -2\gamma/\beta\) is a useful initial guess.

The minimizer over \([t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) is either a root of this quartic in that interval or one of the endpoints. Let \(\tilde{t}_j\) be the choice that minimizes \(f_j(t)\), and define
\(m_j^2 = f_j(\tilde{t}_j)\).

## 7. Final bound for \(RSS_{\min,j}\)

Let \(t_{\max} = 1/low\) and \(A(t_{\max}) = P + t_{\max}\,I\). Then
\(C = \lambda_{\min}(M(t_{\max})) = 1/\lambda_{\max}(A(t_{\max}))^2\).
For all \(t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) with \(t_{\min} = 1/upp\),
\(r_j(t)^{T} M(t)\, r_j(t) \ge C\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2\), hence
\(RSS_j(t) \ge RSS_{ML} + C\,\|r_j(t)\|_2^2\).
Since \(m_j^2 = f_j(\tilde{t}_j)\) minimizes the lower bound (section 6),
\(RSS_{\min,j} \ge RSS_{ML} + C\,m_j^2\). The bound is closed form and valid for all
\(t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# Appendix: Bounding RSS_j(d) Using the ML Decomposition

This appendix explains the identity

\[
RSS_j(d) \;=\; RSS_{ML} \;+\; (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})
\]

and develops a closed form lower bound for

\[
RSS_{\min,j} \;=\; \min_{d \in [low,\,upp]} RSS_j(d).
\]

The key idea is to use the explicit dependence of the tangency point
\(\beta_j(d)\) on the dispersion parameter \(d\).





---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Why the RSS decomposition holds

Let

\[
RSS(\beta) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n w_i (y_i - x_i^{T}\beta)^2,
\qquad
Q \;=\; X^{T} W X,
\]

and let \(\hat{\beta}\) be the weighted least squares (maximum likelihood)
estimate:

\[
\hat{\beta} \;=\; \arg\min_{\beta} RSS(\beta).
\]

Since \(\hat{\beta}\) minimizes \(RSS(\beta)\), the quadratic expansion
around \(\hat{\beta}\) is exact:

\[
RSS(\beta) \;=\; RSS(\hat{\beta})
\;+\;
(\beta - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta - \hat{\beta}).
\]

Therefore, for any face \(j\) and dispersion \(d\),

\[
RSS_j(d)
\;=\;
RSS_{ML}
\;+\;
(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}).
\]

This equality is valid only when \(\hat{\beta}\) is the ML/WLS estimator
computed with the same weights \(w_i\) used in the definition of
\(RSS(\beta)\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Structure of the tangency point beta_j(d)

Let \(X\) be the \(n \times p\) design matrix, \(W = \operatorname{diag}(w_1,\ldots,w_n)\) the weight matrix, and \(\alpha\) the offset vector. Define

\[
\text{base_A} \;=\; X^{T} W\, X \;=\; Q,
\qquad
\text{base_B0} \;=\; X^{T} W\,(\alpha - y).
\]

From the implementation:

\[
A(d) \;=\; P \;+\; \frac{\text{base_A}}{d},
\qquad
B_0(d) \;=\; \frac{\text{base_B0}}{d} \;+\; P\mu,
\]

and

\[
\beta_j(d)
\;=\;
A(d)^{-1}\,\bigl(\bar{c}_j - B_0(d)\bigr).
\]

Introduce the variable

\[
t \;=\; \frac{1}{d},
\qquad
t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}] \;=\; [1/upp,\;1/low].
\]

Define the vectors

\[
a_j \;=\; -\bigl(\text{base_B0} \;+\; \text{base_A}\,\hat{\beta}\bigr),
\qquad
b_j \;=\; \bar{c}_j \;-\; P\mu \;-\; P\hat{\beta}.
\]

Then

\[
r_j(d)
\;=\;
\bar{c}_j \;-\; B_0(d) \;-\; A(d)\hat{\beta}
\;=\;
a_j\,t \;+\; b_j.
\]

Define the "A-B residual"

\[
r_j(d) \;=\; a_j\,t \;+\; b_j.
\]

Using this,

\[
\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}
\;=\;
A(d)^{-1} r_j(d).
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Lower bounding the quadratic form

We want a lower bound on

\[
(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}).
\]

Using \(r_j(d) = \bar{c}_j - B_0(d) - A(d)\hat{\beta}\) and
\(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta} = A(d)^{-1} r_j(d)\), the quadratic form is

\[
(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})
\;=\;
r_j(d)^{T}\, A(d)^{-1} Q\, A(d)^{-1}\, r_j(d)
\;=\;
r_j(d)^{T}\, M(d)\, r_j(d),
\]

where \(M(d) = A(d)^{-1} Q\, A(d)^{-1}\). For any symmetric positive
semi-definite \(M(d)\),

\[
r^{T} M(d)\, r
\;\ge\;
\lambda_{\min}\bigl(M(d)\bigr)\,\|r\|_2^2.
\]

Define

\[
A_{\max} \;=\; P + \frac{\text{base_A}}{low}.
\]

Then \(A_{\max}\) is the maximum of \(A(d)\) over \(d \in [low,\,upp]\) in
the Loewner order (since \(A(d)\) increases as \(1/d\) increases). The
minimum eigenvalue \(\lambda_{\min}(M(d))\) over \(d \in [low,\,upp]\) is
attained at \(d = low\). Define

\[
M_{\min} \;=\; A_{\max}^{-1} Q\, A_{\max}^{-1},
\qquad
C \;=\; \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min}).
\]

Then \(C\) is valid for all \(d \in [low,\,upp]\):

\[
(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})
\;\ge\;
C\,\|r_j(d)\|_2^2.
\]

Then

\[
RSS_j(d)
\;\ge\;
RSS_{ML} \;+\; C\,\|r_j(d)\|_2^2.
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. Closed form minimization of the residual norm

Since

\[
r_j(d) \;=\; a_j\,t \;+\; b_j,
\qquad
t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}],
\]

the squared norm is

\[
\|r_j(d)\|_2^2
\;=\;
\|a_j t + b_j\|_2^2
\;=\;
t^2 \|a_j\|_2^2
\;+\;
2 t\, a_j^{T} b_j
\;+\;
\|b_j\|_2^2.
\]

This is a scalar quadratic in \(t\). The unconstrained minimizer is

\[
t^{*}
\;=\;
-\frac{a_j^{T} b_j}{\|a_j\|_2^2}
\quad\text{(when } a_j \neq 0\text{)}.
\]

Project onto the feasible interval:

\[
\tilde{t}_j
\;=\;
\min\bigl(\max(t^{*},\,t_{\min}),\,t_{\max}\bigr).
\]

Define

\[
m_j^2
\;=\;
\|a_j\,\tilde{t}_j + b_j\|_2^2.
\]

This is the minimum of \(\|r_j(d)\|_2^2\) over all \(d \in [low,\,upp]\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. Final closed form lower bound for RSS_min,j

For all \(d \in [low,\,upp]\),

\[
RSS_j(d)
\;\ge\;
RSS_{ML} \;+\; C\,m_j^2.
\]

Therefore,

\[
RSS_{\min,j}
\;\ge\;
RSS_{ML} \;+\; C\,m_j^2.
\]

Where (\(P\) denotes \(P_2\), the prior contribution to the modified
log-likelihood):

- \(C = \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min})\) with \(M_{\min} = A_{\max}^{-1} Q\, A_{\max}^{-1}\)
- \(A_{\max} = P + \text{base_A}/low\)
- \(a_j = -(\text{base_B0} + \text{base_A}\,\hat{\beta})\)
- \(b_j = \bar{c}_j - P\mu - P\hat{\beta}\)
- \(\tilde{t}_j\) is the projection of \(t^{*}\) onto \([1/upp,\,1/low]\)
- \(m_j^2 = \|a_j\,\tilde{t}_j + b_j\|_2^2\)

This bound is closed form, fast, and valid for all dispersion values in
the allowed interval.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 6. Remarks on the prior, standardization, and the matrix \(M(d)\)

**Prior and default specification.**  
`Prior_Setup()` (default) uses a Zellner-type \(g\)-prior: prior covariance
\(\Sigma = (1-\mathrm{pwt})/\mathrm{pwt} \cdot V_{\mathrm{MLE}}\), so prior
precision \(P = \mathrm{pwt}/(1-\mathrm{pwt}) \cdot Q\), with \(Q = X^{T} W X\).
Thus \(P\) is proportional to \(X^{T} W X\). The default \(\mathrm{pwt}\) is
\(0.01\) for models with fewer than 14 coefficients and \(0.05\) otherwise.

**Standardization.**  
The envelope build may first standardize the model (e.g. via
`glmb_Standardize_Model`). In the pre-standardized model, \(A_1 = P +
X^{T} W X\) (or \(P + Q/d\) at the dispersion mode). The first step
diagonalizes the posterior precision and transforms the design and prior
accordingly; the transformed \(P\) is no longer the original \(P\). The
second step splits the transformed prior into (i) \(\varepsilon\), a scaled
version of its diagonal part, which **remains with the prior**, and (ii)
\(P_3 - \varepsilon\), which is **shifted to the log-likelihood**. The
shifted part is returned as \(P_2\) and passed to
`EnvelopeOrchestrator`, `EnvelopeBuild`, and `EnvelopeDispersionBuild`.
The part that remains in the prior is not used in envelope construction
and is not passed. Then \(A(d) = P_2 + \text{base_A}/d\) is the precision
of the modified log-likelihood, with \(\text{base_A} = X_2^{T} W X_2 =
Q\) in the standardized space.

**Which \(P\) is used in the bound.**  
\(P_2\) is the **prior contribution to the modified log-likelihood** (the
shifted part). The cache holds \(\text{Pmat} = P_2\),
\(\text{base_A} = X_2^{T} W X_2\), and
\(\text{base_B0} = X_2^{T} W (\alpha - y)\). The bound
\(A(d) = P_2 + \text{base_A}/d\) and
\(M_{\min} = A_{\max}^{-1} Q\, A_{\max}^{-1}\) use this \(P_2\). The gradient
vectors \(\bar{c}_j\) defining the envelope faces are computed from the
negative log-posterior of the standardized model, i.e. the modified
log-likelihood (true likelihood plus \(P_2\)) and the remaining prior; they
thus reflect the full effective likelihood.

**Structure of \(Q\) under standardization.**  
**General case:** After the first step, the transformed \(P\) need not be
diagonal. The second step uses \(\varepsilon = \text{scaled}\times
\text{diag}(\text{transformed } P)\) and shifts \(P_3 - \varepsilon\) to the
likelihood. There is no guarantee that the transformed \(P\) is diagonal or
that \(Q \propto I\).

**Zellner g-prior–like case:** When the original \(P \propto Q\), \(P\) and
\(Q\) share the same eigenvectors. The first step diagonalizes both, and in
the intermediate space the transformed \(P\) is not only diagonal but has
**equal values along the diagonal** (proportional to \(I\)). The
second step can then choose \(\varepsilon\) large, keeping most of the prior
with the prior; only a small part \(P_3 - \varepsilon\) is shifted to the
likelihood. Hence \(P_2\) is typically **small relative to**
\(X_2^{T} W X_2 = Q\). The structure is preserved: \(Q \propto I\),
\(P_2 \propto I\), and \(M(d) = A(d)^{-1} Q\, A(d)^{-1}\) is proportional to
\(I\), so the bound \(C = \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min})\) simplifies accordingly.

**Scale choice and impact on \(P_2\).**  
The implementation uses a halving search to find \(\varepsilon\) (divide the
diagonal of the transformed prior by 2 until \(P_3 - \varepsilon\) is positive
definite), without the additive refinement phase described in @Nygren2006.
This tends to shift more of the prior into the likelihood
and thus yields a larger \(P_2\). That choice favors numerical stability when
the data precision \(Q = X^{T} W X\) is rank deficient: adding meaningful
prior precision to the modified likelihood improves conditioning and
regularizes the model. Even so, with the default prior
(\(\mathrm{pwt} = 0.01\) or \(0.05\)), the original \(P\) is only 1–5% of
\(Q\), so \(P_2\) remains **small relative to** \(X^{T} W X\) in typical use.

**Tightness of the bound.**  
When \(Q \propto I\) and \(P \propto I\), the exact quadratic term equals
\((q/a(d)^2)\,\|r_j(d)\|^2\) with \(a(d) = p + q/d\). The constant
\(C = \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min})\) equals \(q/a(low)^2\), the minimum of
\(q/a(d)^2\) over \(d \in [low,\,upp]\). Hence the inequality
\((\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}) \ge C\,\|r_j(d)\|^2\)
holds with **equality at \(d = low\)**; the bound is tight at the lower
endpoint. For \(d > low\), the true quadratic is strictly larger than
\(C\,\|r_j\|^2\), so the bound remains valid but is conservative.

**Anchor \(d^*_1\).**  
The dispersion anchor \(d^*_1 = \mathrm{rate}_3 / (\mathrm{shape}_2 - 1)\) is
the posterior mode of the inverse-gamma for dispersion. The interval
\([low,\,upp]\) is typically chosen so that \(low < d^*_1 < upp\), centering
the bounds around the posterior mode.

**Examples: how \(M(d)\) behaves.**  
With \(M(d) = A(d)^{-1} Q\, A(d)^{-1}\) and
\(A(d) = P + Q/d\) (where \(P\) denotes \(P_2\)):

1. **Strong prior (\(P\) large):** \(A(d) \approx P\) for moderate \(d\),
   so \(M(d) \approx P^{-1} Q\, P^{-1}\). The bound \(C = \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min})\)
   reflects both prior and likelihood.
2. **Weak prior (\(P\) small):** \(A(d) \approx Q/d\), so
   \(M(d) \approx d^2\, Q^{-1} Q\, Q^{-1} = d^2\, Q^{-1}\). Then
   \(\lambda_{\min}(M(d)) \approx d^2 / \lambda_{\max}(Q)\).
3. **Standardized model (\(P = I\)):** \(A(d) = I + Q/d\), and \(M(d)\) is
   determined by the diagonalized \(Q\).

**Limiting case \(P \to 0\).**  
As the prior contribution to the modified log-likelihood tends to the
zero matrix, \(A(d) = P + Q/d \to Q/d\).
Then \(A_{\max} \to Q/low\), so
\(M_{\min} = A_{\max}^{-1} Q\, A_{\max}^{-1}
\to (Q/low)^{-1} Q\, (Q/low)^{-1}
= low^2\, Q^{-1}\).
Thus \(\lambda_{\min}(M_{\min}) \to low^2 / \lambda_{\max}(Q)\). The constant
\(C\) no longer involves the prior; the bound depends only on the likelihood
information \(Q\) and the lower dispersion bound \(low\). In that limit, the
Appendix A bound reduces to
\((\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta})
\ge (low^2/\lambda_{\max}(Q))\,\|r_j(d)\|_2^2\), valid for \(d \ge low\).


# Appendix B: UB2 Minimization and Bounds

This appendix addresses the minimization of

\[
\mathrm{UB2}_j(d)
\;=\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,\bigl(\mathrm{RSS}_j(d) - \mathrm{RSS\_Min}\bigr)
\quad\text{over } d \in [low,\,upp].
\]

Using the RSS decomposition from Appendix A,

\[
\mathrm{RSS}(\beta) \;=\; \mathrm{RSS}(\hat{\beta})
\;+\;
(\beta - \hat{\beta})^{T} Q (\beta - \hat{\beta}),
\]

write both \(\mathrm{RSS}_j(d)\) and \(\mathrm{RSS\_Min}\) as a sum of two terms. The
first term is identical and cancels in the difference.

For face \(j\) at dispersion \(d\), \(\beta_j(d)\) is the tangency point:

\[
\mathrm{RSS}_j(d)
\;=\;
\mathrm{RSS}(\hat{\beta})
\;+\;
\bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)^{T} Q \bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr).
\]

For \(\mathrm{RSS\_Min}\), the quadratic component at the global minimizer is
bounded below by \(C\,m_{\min}^2\) (Appendix A: \(C = \lambda_{\min}(M_{\min})\)
and \(m_{\min}^2 = \min_j m_j^2\) with \(m_j^2 = \min_d \|r_j(d)\|_2^2\)). Using
that bound for the quadratic part,

\[
\mathrm{RSS\_Min}
\;=\;
\mathrm{RSS}(\hat{\beta})
\;+\;
C\,m_{\min}^2.
\]

The first terms cancel, so

\[
\mathrm{RSS}_j(d) - \mathrm{RSS\_Min}
\;=\;
\bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)^{T} Q \bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)
\;-\;
C\,m_{\min}^2,
\]

and thus

\[
\mathrm{UB2}_j(d)
\;=\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
\bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)^{T} Q \bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta}\bigr)
\;-\;
C\,m_{\min}^2
\Bigr].
\]

With \(r_j(d) = \bar{c}_j - B_0(d) - A(d)\hat{\beta}\) and
\(\beta_j(d) - \hat{\beta} = A(d)^{-1} r_j(d)\), the quadratic equals
\(r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d)\) for \(M(d) = A(d)^{-1} Q\, A(d)^{-1}\) (Appendix A), so

\[
\mathrm{UB2}_j(d)
\;=\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d) \;-\; C\,m_{\min}^2
\Bigr].
\]

Introduce the parameterized matrix
\(\tilde{A}(d,P) = P + (1/d)\,\text{base_A}\)
and
\(\tilde{M}(d,P) = \tilde{A}(d,P)^{-1} Q\, \tilde{A}(d,P)^{-1}\).
Note that \(A(d) = \tilde{A}(d,P)\) and \(M(d) = \tilde{M}(d,P)\) with the prior precision \(P\).
Then we can decompose

\[
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d) \;-\; C\,m_{\min}^2
\Bigr]
\;=\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T} \tilde{M}(d,0)\, r_j(d) \;-\; C\,m_{\min}^2
\Bigr]
\;+\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T}\,
\bigl[\tilde{M}(d,P) - \tilde{M}(d,0)\bigr]\,
r_j(d)
\Bigr].
\]

**Remark (simplification when \(P = 0\)).**  
For \(P = 0\), \(\tilde{A}(d,0) = (1/d)\,Q\), so
\(\tilde{M}(d,0) = \tilde{A}(d,0)^{-1} Q\, \tilde{A}(d,0)^{-1} = d^2\,Q^{-1}\).
With \(t = 1/d\) and \(r_j = a_j\,t + b_j\), the first term becomes
\[
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j^{T} \tilde{M}(d,0)\, r_j \;-\; C\,m_{\min}^2
\Bigr]
\;=\;
\frac{r_j^{T} Q^{-1} r_j}{2\,t} \;-\; \frac{C\,m_{\min}^2\,t}{2}
\;=\;
\frac{(\alpha' - C\,m_{\min}^2)\,t^2 + \beta'\,t + \gamma'}{2\,t},
\]
where \(\alpha' = a_j^{T} Q^{-1} a_j\), \(\beta' = 2\,a_j^{T} Q^{-1} b_j\),
\(\gamma' = b_j^{T} Q^{-1} b_j\). Thus the first term is a **quadratic in \(t\) divided by a single term in \(t\)** (the denominator \(2t\)).

**Remark (monotonicity and critical points).**  
Introduce \(\kappa = \alpha' - C\,m_{\min}^2\) and \(\zeta = \gamma' = b_j^{T} Q^{-1} b_j\).
Since \(Q^{-1}\) is positive definite, \(\zeta \ge 0\) (and \(\zeta > 0\) when \(b_j \ne 0\)).
Then \(f(t) = (\kappa/2)t + (\beta'/2) + \zeta/(2t)\) and
\(f'(t) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\kappa - \zeta/t^2\bigr)\).
The behavior of \(f\) is determined by the sign of \(\kappa\). We have two cases:

- **(i)** \(\kappa > 0\): there is a unique critical point \(t^* = \sqrt{\zeta/\kappa}\);
  \(f''(t) = \zeta/t^3 \ge 0\) for \(t > 0\), so \(t^*\) is a global minimum; \(f\)
  decreases on \((0,t^*)\) and increases on \((t^*,\infty)\).
- **(ii)** \(\kappa \le 0\): \(f'(t) < 0\) for all \(t > 0\) (assuming \(\zeta > 0\)), so
  \(f\) is strictly decreasing.

In both cases, the function obtains a unique minimum over the interval.


**Conjecture (Bounding of second term from below)**

\[
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T}\,
\bigl[\tilde{M}(d,P) - \tilde{M}(d,0)\bigr]\,
r_j(d)
\Bigr]
\;\ge\;
\frac{1}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
r_j(d)^{T}\,
\bigl[\tilde{M}(\mathrm{upp},P) - \tilde{M}(\mathrm{upp},0)\bigr]\,
r_j(d)
\Bigr].
\]

Define \(D(d) = \tilde{M}(d,P) - \tilde{M}(d,0)\). The inequality holds for all \(r_j(d)\) iff
\(D(d) \succeq D(\mathrm{upp})\) when \(d \le \mathrm{upp}\), i.e. \(D(d)\) is decreasing in \(d\) (Loewner order).
Since \(1/(2d) > 0\), it suffices to show that \(r^{T} D(d)\, r \ge r^{T} D(\mathrm{upp})\, r\) for all \(r\),
which is equivalent to \(D(d) \succeq D(\mathrm{upp})\).

**Proofs (special cases)**

Let \(\tilde{A}(d,P) = P + (1/d)\,Q\), \(\tilde{M}(d,P) = \tilde{A}(d,P)^{-1} Q\, \tilde{A}(d,P)^{-1}\),
and \(\tilde{M}(d,0) = d^2\,Q^{-1}\). Then \(D(d) = \tilde{M}(d,P) - d^2\,Q^{-1}\).
All proofs use \(t = 1/d\) and \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,Q\).

---

**Special Case 1: Univariate.** \(P > 0\), \(Q > 0\) scalars.

Then \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,Q\), \(\tilde{M}(d,P) = Q\,/\,\tilde{A}(t)^2 = Q/(P+tQ)^2\),
\(\tilde{M}(d,0) = d^2/Q = 1/(t^2 Q)\), so
\[
D(d) \;=\; \frac{Q}{(P + tQ)^2} \;-\; \frac{1}{t^2\,Q}.
\]
Differentiate w.r.t. \(t\) (with \(dt/dd = -1/d^2 < 0\)): 
\[
\frac{dD(d)}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{2\,Q^2}{(P+tQ)^3} \;+\; \frac{2}{t^3\,Q}
\;=\; 2\Bigl(\frac{1}{t^3 Q} \;-\; \frac{Q^2}{(P+tQ)^3}\Bigr).
\]
So \(dD(d)/dt \ge 0\) iff \(\displaystyle\frac{1}{t^3 Q} \ge \frac{Q^2}{(P+tQ)^3}\)
iff \((P+tQ)^3 \ge t^3 Q^3\) iff \(P + tQ \ge tQ\), i.e. \(P \ge 0\), which holds.
Thus \(D\) increases in \(t\), so \(D\) decreases in \(d\) (since \(t = 1/d\)). Hence
\(D(d) \ge D(\mathrm{upp})\) for \(d \le \mathrm{upp}\).

---

**Special Case 2: \(P = k\,Q\)** for some \(k > 0\).

Then \(P\) and \(Q\) commute. We have \(\tilde{A}(t) = (k + t)\,Q\), so
\(\tilde{A}(t)^{-1} = Q^{-1}/(k+t)\) and
\[
\tilde{M}(d,P) \;=\; \frac{Q^{-1}\,Q\,Q^{-1}}{(k+t)^2} \;=\; \frac{Q^{-1}}{(k+t)^2}.
\]
Also \(\tilde{M}(d,0) = d^2\,Q^{-1} = Q^{-1}/t^2\). Thus
\[
D(d) \;=\; Q^{-1}\Bigl(\frac{1}{(k+t)^2} \;-\; \frac{1}{t^2}\Bigr),
\]
and \(Q^{-1} \succ 0\) is a positive constant matrix. The scalar factor
\(\frac{1}{(k+t)^2} - \frac{1}{t^2}\) is the same as in the univariate case
(with \(P = k\,Q\) and \(Q\) normalized to 1). It is decreasing in \(d\) (increasing in \(t\))
by the same argument as Case 1. Hence \(D(d) \succeq D(\mathrm{upp})\) for \(d \le \mathrm{upp}\).

---

**Special Case 3: \(P\) and \(Q\) both diagonal.**

Let \(P = \mathrm{diag}(p_1,\ldots,p_n)\), \(Q = \mathrm{diag}(q_1,\ldots,q_n)\) with \(p_i, q_i > 0\).
Then \(\tilde{A}(t) = \mathrm{diag}(p_1 + t\,q_1, \ldots, p_n + t\,q_n)\) is diagonal, and
\[
\tilde{M}(d,P)_{ii} \;=\; \frac{q_i}{(p_i + t\,q_i)^2}, \qquad
\tilde{M}(d,0)_{ii} \;=\; \frac{q_i}{t^2\,q_i^2} \;=\; \frac{q_i}{(t\,q_i)^2}.
\]
Both terms have \(q_i\) in the numerator and a squared denominator; the first uses
\((p_i + t\,q_i)^2\) and the second uses \((t\,q_i)^2\). So \(D(d)\) is diagonal with
\[
D(d)_{ii} \;=\; \frac{q_i}{(p_i + t\,q_i)^2} \;-\; \frac{q_i}{(t\,q_i)^2}
\;=\; q_i\Bigl(\frac{1}{(p_i + t\,q_i)^2} \;-\; \frac{1}{(t\,q_i)^2}\Bigr).
\]
Differentiating with respect to \(t\),
\[
\frac{dD_{ii}}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{2\,q_i^2}{(p_i + t\,q_i)^3} \;+\; \frac{2}{t^3\,q_i}
\;=\; 2\Bigl(\frac{1}{t^3\,q_i} \;-\; \frac{q_i^2}{(p_i + t\,q_i)^3}\Bigr).
\]
Each diagonal entry is the univariate derivative; \(dD_{ii}/dt \ge 0\) iff
\((p_i + t\,q_i)^3 \ge t^3\,q_i^3\), i.e. \(p_i + t\,q_i \ge t\,q_i\), which holds since \(p_i > 0\).
Hence \(D\) is increasing in \(t\), decreasing in \(d\), and \(D(d) \succeq D(\mathrm{upp})\) for \(d \le \mathrm{upp}\).

---

**Special Case 4: Standardization at \(d_1^*\).**

Let \(d_1^* \in [\mathrm{low},\,\mathrm{upp}]\) be the dispersion used in standardization.
At \(t^* = 1/d_1^*\), the posterior precision \(\tilde{A}(t^*) = P + Q/d_1^*\) is diagonal
(after the L2/L3 transforms). The diagonal elements are \(a_k^* = P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk} > 0\). Then
\(\tilde{A}(t^*)^{-1}\) is diagonal with \((1/a_k^*)\), and
\(B^* = Q^{1/2}\,\tilde{A}(t^*)^{-1}\,Q^{1/2}\) need not be diagonal; its elements are
\[
(B^*)_{ij} \;=\; \sum_k \frac{(Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jk}}{P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk}}.
\]
The derivative uses \((B^*)^3\), not \((B^*)^2\): \(dD/dt = 2\,Q^{-1/2}(I/(t^*)^3 - (B^*)^3)\,Q^{-1/2}\).
The \((i,j)\)-element of \((B^*)^3\) is
\[
\bigl((B^*)^3\bigr)_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{\ell,r} (B^*)_{i\ell}\,(B^*)_{\ell r}\,(B^*)_{r j}
\;=\; \sum_{k,m,n,\ell,r}
\frac{(Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell k}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell m}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rm}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jn}}
{(P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk})(P_{mm} + t^*\,Q_{mm})(P_{nn} + t^*\,Q_{nn})},
\]
so the derivative at \(t^*\) is \(2\,Q^{-1/2}\,M\,Q^{-1/2}\), where \(M\) has \((i,j)\)-element
\[
M_{ij} \;=\;
\sum_{k,m,n,\ell,r} (Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell k}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell m}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rm}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jn}
\Biggl[
\frac{1}{(t^*\,Q_{kk})(t^*\,Q_{mm})(t^*\,Q_{nn})}
\;-\;
\frac{1}{(P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk})(P_{mm} + t^*\,Q_{mm})(P_{nn} + t^*\,Q_{nn})}
\Biggr].
\]
Since \(Q^{-1/2} \succ 0\), \(dD/dt \succeq 0\) at \(t^*\) iff \(M \succeq 0\). Then \(D\) is increasing in \(t\)
at the anchor, hence decreasing in \(d\) at \(d_1^*\).

**Comparison with Special Case 3.** When \(P = \mathrm{diag}(p_1,\ldots,p_n)\) and
\(Q = \mathrm{diag}(q_1,\ldots,q_n)\), \(Q^{1/2}\) is diagonal with \((Q^{1/2})_{ik} = \sqrt{q_i}\,\delta_{ik}\).
Then
\[
(B^*)_{ij} \;=\; \sum_k \frac{(Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jk}}{P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk}}
\;=\; \sum_k \frac{\sqrt{q_i}\,\delta_{ik}\,\sqrt{q_j}\,\delta_{jk}}{p_k + t^*\,q_k}.
\]
The product \(\delta_{ik}\,\delta_{jk}\) is 1 only when \(i = j = k\); otherwise it is 0. So
\(B^*\) is diagonal with
\[
(B^*)_{ii} \;=\; \frac{q_i}{p_i + t^*\,q_i}, \qquad (B^*)_{ij} \;=\; 0 \;\; \text{for } i \ne j.
\]

For \((B^*)^3\), substitute \((Q^{1/2})_{ab} = \sqrt{q_a}\,\delta_{ab}\) into
\[
\bigl((B^*)^3\bigr)_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{k,m,n,\ell,r}
\frac{(Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell k}\,(Q^{1/2})_{\ell m}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rm}\,(Q^{1/2})_{rn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jn}}
{(P_{kk} + t^*\,Q_{kk})(P_{mm} + t^*\,Q_{mm})(P_{nn} + t^*\,Q_{nn})}.
\]
The numerator is
\(\sqrt{q_i}\,\delta_{ik}\,\sqrt{q_{\ell}}\,\delta_{\ell k}\,\sqrt{q_{\ell}}\,\delta_{\ell m}\,
\sqrt{q_r}\,\delta_{rm}\,\sqrt{q_r}\,\delta_{rn}\,\sqrt{q_j}\,\delta_{jn}\).
For this to be non-zero, \(\delta_{ik}\,\delta_{\ell k}\,\delta_{\ell m}\,\delta_{rm}\,\delta_{rn}\,\delta_{jn} = 1\),
so \(i = k\), \(\ell = k\), \(\ell = m\), \(r = m\), \(r = n\), and \(j = n\). Hence \(i = \ell = k = m = r = n = j\):
all indices collapse. The sum reduces to a single term with \(k = m = n = i\), giving
\[
\bigl((B^*)^3\bigr)_{ii} \;=\; \frac{q_i^3}{(p_i + t^*\,q_i)^3}, \qquad
\bigl((B^*)^3\bigr)_{ij} \;=\; 0 \;\; \text{for } i \ne j.
\]

Since \(M = I/(t^*)^3 - (B^*)^3\), in the diagonal case \(M\) is diagonal with
\[
M_{ii} \;=\; \frac{1}{(t^*)^3} \;-\; \frac{q_i^3}{(p_i + t^*\,q_i)^3}, \qquad
M_{ij} \;=\; 0 \;\; \text{for } i \ne j.
\]
This agrees with Special Case 3: \(dD_{ii}/dt = 2\,Q^{-1/2} M\,Q^{-1/2}\) gives
\(dD_{ii}/dt = 2\,M_{ii}/q_i = 2\bigl(1/(t^3\,q_i) - q_i^2/(p_i + t\,q_i)^3\bigr)\).

**Two-dimensional case.** For \(p = 2\), indices \(k,m,n,\ell,r \in \{1,2\}\).
Write the bracketed factor
\[
\Delta_{kmn} \;=\; \frac{1}{(t^*\,Q_{kk})(t^*\,Q_{mm})(t^*\,Q_{nn})}
\;-\; \frac{1}{(P_{kk}+t^*\,Q_{kk})(P_{mm}+t^*\,Q_{mm})(P_{nn}+t^*\,Q_{nn})},
\]
and use \(\sum_{\ell} (Q^{1/2})_{\ell k}(Q^{1/2})_{\ell m} = Q_{km}\) and
\(\sum_r (Q^{1/2})_{rm}(Q^{1/2})_{rn} = Q_{mn}\) (since \(Q = (Q^{1/2})^2\)). Then
\[
M_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{k,m,n \,\in\, \{1,2\}}
(Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jn}\;\Delta_{kmn},
\]
a sum over 8 terms \((k,m,n) \in \{1,2\}^3\).
With \(M_{12} = M_{21}\) (since \(M\) is symmetric), the eigenvalues of \(M\) are
\[
\mu_\pm \;=\; \frac{M_{11} + M_{22} \;\pm\; \sqrt{(M_{11}-M_{22})^2 + 4\,M_{12}^2}}{2},
\]
where \(M_{11}\), \(M_{12}\), \(M_{22}\) are given explicitly by the sum above (indices \(i,j,k,m,n,\ell,r \in \{1,2\}\)).
\(M \succeq 0\) iff \(\mu_- \ge 0\), i.e.
\(M_{11} + M_{22} \ge \sqrt{(M_{11}-M_{22})^2 + 4\,M_{12}^2}\).
With the 2D form \(M_{ij} = \sum_{k,m,n} (Q^{1/2})_{ik}\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{jn}\,\Delta_{kmn}\),
\[
\sum_{k,m,n} \Bigl[(Q^{1/2})_{1k}\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{1n}
\;+\; (Q^{1/2})_{2k}\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{2n}\Bigr]\,\Delta_{kmn}
\]
\[
\ge \sqrt{
\Biggl[\sum_{k,m,n} \Bigl((Q^{1/2})_{1k}\,(Q^{1/2})_{1n} - (Q^{1/2})_{2k}\,(Q^{1/2})_{2n}\Bigr)\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,\Delta_{kmn}\Biggr]^2
\;+\;
4\Biggl[\sum_{k,m,n} (Q^{1/2})_{1k}\,Q_{km}\,Q_{mn}\,(Q^{1/2})_{2n}\,\Delta_{kmn}\Biggr]^2
},
\]
where all sums run over \(k,m,n \in \{1,2\}\).

---

**Special Case 5: General case.**

Let \(B = Q^{1/2}\,\tilde{A}(t)^{-1}\,Q^{1/2}\) with \(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,Q\). Then
\[
\frac{dD}{dt} \;=\; 2\,Q^{-1/2}\Bigl(\frac{I}{t^3} \;-\; B^3\Bigr)Q^{-1/2}.
\]
So \(dD/dt \succeq 0\) (hence \(D\) decreasing in \(d\)) iff \(B^3 \preceq I/t^3\),
i.e. all eigenvalues of \(B\) satisfy \(\lambda(B) \le t^{-1}\). For the general case
(arbitrary positive definite \(P\) and \(Q\)), this eigenvalue condition is not
obvious. The conjecture remains open; numerical checks support it.

---

**Special Case 6: \(Q = I\), \(P \succ 0\) arbitrary.**

Let \(Q = I\) and \(P\) be an arbitrary positive definite matrix. Then
\(\tilde{A}(t) = P + t\,I\), so \(\tilde{A}(t)^{-1} = (P + t\,I)^{-1}\), and
\[
B \;=\; Q^{1/2}\,\tilde{A}(t)^{-1}\,Q^{1/2} \;=\; (P + t\,I)^{-1}.
\]
Hence \(B\) and \(P + t\,I\) share eigenvectors; if \(\lambda_i(P) > 0\) is an eigenvalue
of \(P\), then \(P + t\,I\) has eigenvalue \(\lambda_i(P) + t\), and \(B\) has eigenvalue
\(1/(\lambda_i(P) + t)\). Thus \(B^3\) has eigenvalues \(1/(\lambda_i(P) + t)^3\), and
\(M = I/t^3 - B^3\) has eigenvalues
\[
\mu_i \;=\; \frac{1}{t^3} \;-\; \frac{1}{(\lambda_i(P) + t)^3}.
\]
For \(M \succeq 0\) we need \(\mu_i \ge 0\) for all \(i\), i.e.
\(\displaystyle\frac{1}{t^3} \ge \frac{1}{(\lambda_i(P) + t)^3}\),
or \((\lambda_i(P) + t)^3 \ge t^3\), hence \(\lambda_i(P) + t \ge t\), i.e. \(\lambda_i(P) \ge 0\).
Since \(P \succ 0\), all \(\lambda_i(P) > 0\), so \(\mu_i > 0\) for all \(i\). Thus \(M \succ 0\),
\(dD/dt \succ 0\), and \(D\) is strictly decreasing in \(d\). The conjecture holds.

**General result by reparametrization.**  
As long as \(Q = X^{T} W X\) is positive definite, the model can be reparametrized to
an equivalent model in which \(\tilde{X}^{T}\tilde{W}\tilde{X} = I\). Define
\(\beta = Q^{-1/2}\,\tilde{\beta}\). Then the precision in \(\tilde{\beta}\)-space is
\(\tilde{A}(d) = Q^{-1/2}(P + Q/d) Q^{-1/2} = \tilde{P} + I/d\), with
\(\tilde{P} = Q^{-1/2}\,P\,Q^{-1/2} \succ 0\). In this space, the "data precision" is
\(\tilde{Q} = I\) and the prior is \(\tilde{P}\); we are in the setting of Special Case 6.
The conjecture holds there, i.e. \(\tilde{D}(d)\) is decreasing in \(d\). The
reparametrization is an invertible linear map, so the Loewner order is preserved:
\(D(d) \succeq D(\mathrm{upp})\) in the original space iff
\(\tilde{D}(d) \succeq \tilde{D}(\mathrm{upp})\) in the reparametrized space. Hence the
conjecture holds in the general case whenever \(Q \succ 0\) and \(P \succ 0\).


Define \(\mathrm{UB2b}_j(d)\) by replacing \(M(d)\) with \(C\) (and factoring \(C\) out):

\[
\mathrm{UB2b}_j(d)
\;=\;
\frac{C}{2d}\,
\Bigl[
\|r_j(d)\|_2^2 \;-\; m_{\min}^2
\Bigr].
\]

**Rewrite in \(t = 1/d\).** From Appendix A, \(r_j(d) = a_j\,t + b_j\) and
\(\|r_j(d)\|_2^2 = \alpha\,t^2 + \beta\,t + \gamma\) with
\(\alpha = \|a_j\|_2^2\), \(\beta = 2\,a_j^{T}b_j\), \(\gamma = \|b_j\|_2^2\).
Since \(1/(2d) = t/2\),

\[
\mathrm{UB2b}_j(d)
\;=\;
\frac{C\,t}{2}\,
\Bigl[
(\alpha\,t^2 + \beta\,t + \gamma) \;-\; m_{\min}^2
\Bigr]
\;=\;
\frac{C}{2}\,
\Bigl[
\alpha\,t^3 \;+\; \beta\,t^2 \;+\; (\gamma - m_{\min}^2)\,t
\Bigr].
\]

Thus \(\mathrm{UB2b}_j\) is **cubic in \(t\)** over
\(t \in [t_{\min},\,t_{\max}]\) with \(t_{\min} = 1/upp\) and
\(t_{\max} = 1/low\).

**Derivatives and critical points.**  
Write the bracketed cubic as
\(f(t) = \alpha\,t^3 + \beta\,t^2 + (\gamma - m_{\min}^2)\,t\), so
\(\mathrm{UB2b}_j(d) = (C/2)\,f(t)\). Then

\[
f'(t) \;=\; 3\alpha\,t^2 \;+\; 2\beta\,t \;+\; (\gamma - m_{\min}^2),
\qquad
f''(t) \;=\; 6\alpha\,t \;+\; 2\beta.
\]

Setting \(f'(t) = 0\) gives the quadratic
\(3\alpha\,t^2 + 2\beta\,t + (\gamma - m_{\min}^2) = 0\). Using the
quadratic formula with discriminant
\(D = \beta^2 - 3\alpha\,(\gamma - m_{\min}^2)\):

\[
t_{\pm}
\;=\;
\frac{-2\beta \;\pm\; \sqrt{4\beta^2 - 12\alpha\,(\gamma - m_{\min}^2)}}{6\alpha}
\;=\;
\frac{-\beta \;\pm\; \sqrt{D}}{3\alpha}.
\]

The two critical points are
\(t_1 = (-\beta + \sqrt{D})/(3\alpha)\) and
\(t_2 = (-\beta - \sqrt{D})/(3\alpha)\). Evaluating the second derivative:

\[
f''(t_1) \;=\; 6\alpha\,\frac{-\beta + \sqrt{D}}{3\alpha} \;+\; 2\beta
\;=\; 2\sqrt{D}
\quad\text{and}\quad
f''(t_2) \;=\; -2\sqrt{D}.
\]

When \(D > 0\), \(f''(t_1) > 0\) so \(t_1\) is a **local minimum**, and
\(f''(t_2) < 0\) so \(t_2\) is a **local maximum**. When \(D = 0\), the
two critical points coincide (inflection point).

**Remark.** For all \(d \in [low,\,upp]\),
\(\mathrm{UB2}_j(d) \ge \mathrm{UB2b}_j(d)\).  
*Proof:* The eigenvalue bound (Appendix A) gives
\(r^{T} M(d)\, r \ge C\,\|r\|_2^2\) for all \(r\) and \(d\). Thus
\(r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d) \ge C\,\|r_j(d)\|_2^2\), so the numerator of
\(\mathrm{UB2}_j(d)\) satisfies
\(r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d) - C\,m_{\min}^2
\ge C\,\|r_j(d)\|_2^2 - C\,m_{\min}^2
= C\,(\|r_j(d)\|_2^2 - m_{\min}^2)\), which is the numerator of
\(\mathrm{UB2b}_j(d)\). Since \(1/(2d) > 0\), the inequality follows.

The objective is a product of two factors: \(1/(2d)\), which is decreasing in
\(d\) (minimized at \(d = upp\)), and the excess quadratic form in the
brackets, which achieves its minimum elsewhere (possibly at \(d = low\) or an
interior point), so the product introduces a trade-off. We consider
computable lower bounds and good starting values for numerical optimization.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## Derivatives of UB2 (exact, not the bound)

Since \(\beta_j(d)\) is a function of \(d\), we derive the derivatives of
\(\mathrm{UB2}_j(d)\) with respect to \(d\). Write

\[
\mathrm{UB2}_j(d) \;=\; \frac{\psi(d)}{2d},
\quad
\psi(d) \;=\; \bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat\beta\bigr)^{T} Q \bigl(\beta_j(d) - \hat\beta\bigr) - C\,m_{\min}^2 \;=\; r_j(d)^{T} M(d)\, r_j(d) - C\,m_{\min}^2.
\]

**Derivatives of \(A\), \(B_0\), \(A^{-1}\):**

\[
\frac{dA}{dd} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{d^2},
\quad
\frac{dB_0}{dd} \;=\; -\frac{\mathrm{base\_B0}}{d^2},
\quad
\frac{dA^{-1}}{dd} \;=\; \frac{1}{d^2}\,A^{-1}Q\,A^{-1}.
\]

**Derivative of \(\beta_j\):**

\[
\frac{d\beta_j}{dd} \;=\; \frac{1}{d^2}\,A^{-1}\bigl( Q\,\beta_j + \mathrm{base\_B0} \bigr).
\]

**Derivative of \(r_j\):**  
Using \(r_j = \bar{c}_j - B_0 - A\hat\beta\) and \(a_j = -(\mathrm{base\_B0} + Q\hat\beta)\),

\[
\frac{dr_j}{dd} \;=\; -\frac{a_j}{d^2}.
\]

**Derivative of \(M(d) = A^{-1}Q\,A^{-1}\):**

\[
\frac{dM}{dd} \;=\; \frac{2}{d^2}\,A^{-1}Q\,M.
\]

**Derivative of \(\psi\):**

\[
\frac{d\psi}{dd} \;=\; 2\,r_j^{T} M\,\frac{dr_j}{dd} + r_j^{T}\,\frac{dM}{dd}\,r_j
\;=\; \frac{2}{d^2}\,r_j^{T} M\,\bigl( Q\beta_j + \mathrm{base\_B0} \bigr).
\]

**First derivative of UB2:**

\[
\frac{d\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dd} \;=\; \frac{\psi'(d)\,d - \psi(d)}{2d^2}.
\]

**Second derivative of UB2:**

\[
\frac{d^2\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dd^2} \;=\; \frac{\psi''(d)\,d^2 - 2\bigl(\psi'(d)\,d - \psi(d)\bigr)}{2d^3},
\]

where \(\psi''\) is obtained by differentiating \(\psi'\) (using \(dr_j/dd\), \(dM/dd\), \(d\beta_j/dd\) above).

**Derivatives in \(t = 1/d\).**  
Parametrize with \(t = 1/d\), so \(d = 1/t\) and \(dd/dt = -1/t^2\). Write
\[
g(t) \;=\; \mathrm{UB2}(1/t) \;=\; \frac{t\,\psi(1/t)}{2}.
\]
By the product rule,
\[
\frac{dg}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\Bigl[ \psi(1/t) \;+\; t\,\frac{d\psi}{dt} \Bigr].
\]
With \(\tau = 1/t\), we have \(d\psi/dt = \psi'(\tau)\,d\tau/dt = \psi'(\tau)\cdot(-1/t^2)\), so
\[
\frac{dg}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\Bigl[ \psi\Bigl(\frac{1}{t}\Bigr) \;-\; \frac{1}{t}\,\psi'\Bigl(\frac{1}{t}\Bigr) \Bigr].
\]
In terms of \(d = 1/t\):
\[
\frac{d\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\psi(d) - \psi'(d)\,d}{2} \;=\; -\frac{\psi'(d)\,d - \psi(d)}{2}.
\]
This agrees with the chain rule: \(d/dt = (dd/dt)\,d/dd = -d^2\,(d/dd)\), so
\(d(\mathrm{UB2})/dt = -d^2\cdot(\psi'd - \psi)/(2d^2) = -(\psi'd - \psi)/2\).

For the second derivative, differentiate
\(\frac{dg}{dt} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[ \psi(1/t) - t^{-1}\psi'(1/t) \bigr]\) again. With
\(\tau = 1/t\) and \(d\tau/dt = -1/t^2\):
\[
\frac{d^2g}{dt^2}
\;=\; \frac{1}{2}\Bigl[
\psi'(1/t)\cdot\Bigl(-\frac{1}{t^2}\Bigr)
\;-\; \Bigl(-\frac{1}{t^2}\Bigr)\psi'(1/t)
\;-\; \frac{1}{t}\,\psi''(1/t)\cdot\Bigl(-\frac{1}{t^2}\Bigr)
\Bigr].
\]
The first two terms cancel. Thus
\[
\frac{d^2g}{dt^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\psi''(1/t)}{t^3}.
\]
In terms of \(d = 1/t\), using \(t^3 = 1/d^3\):
\[
\frac{d^2\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dt^2} \;=\; \frac{\psi''(d)}{2\,d^3}.
\]
At a minimizer in \(d\), \(\psi'd = \psi\), so \(d(\mathrm{UB2})/dt = 0\). At that point,
\(d^2(\mathrm{UB2})/dt^2 = \psi''/(2d^3)\). Whether the second derivative is positive
depends on \(\psi''\); see below for the explicit formula.

**Formula for \(\psi''\) (for use in \(d^2\mathrm{UB2}/dt^2\)).**  
Let \(u = Q\beta_j + \mathrm{base\_B0}\). From \(\psi'(d) = (2/d^2)\,r_j^{T} M\,u\),

\[
\psi''(d)
\;=\;
\frac{2}{d^4}\Bigl[
-a_j^{T} M\,u
\;+\; 2\,r_j^{T} A^{-1}Q\,M\,u
\;+\; r_j^{T} M\,Q\,A^{-1}u
\Bigr]
\;-\; \frac{4}{d^3}\,r_j^{T} M\,u,
\]

with \(A\), \(M\), \(r_j\), \(\beta_j\), \(u\) evaluated at \(d\). In terms of \(t = 1/d\),
use \(d = 1/t\), \(r_j = a_j\,t + b_j\), \(A = P + Q\,t\), \(M = A^{-1}Q\,A^{-1}\), and
\(\psi''(1/t)\) in the formula above. Then

\[
\frac{d^2\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dt^2}
\;=\;
\frac{\psi''(1/t)}{2\,t^3}.
\]

To determine the sign of \(d^2\mathrm{UB2}/dt^2\) at a given \(t\), compute
\(\psi''(1/t)\) from the expression above; the second derivative in \(t\) is
positive where \(\psi''(1/t) > 0\).

**Summary table:**

| Quantity | Expression |
|----------|------------|
| \(\frac{d\beta_j}{dd}\) | \(\frac{1}{d^2}A^{-1}(Q\beta_j + \mathrm{base\_B0})\) |
| \(\frac{dr_j}{dd}\) | \(-\frac{a_j}{d^2}\) |
| \(\frac{dM}{dd}\) | \(\frac{2}{d^2}A^{-1}Q\,M\) |
| \(\psi'(d)\) | \(\frac{2}{d^2}\,r_j^{T} M\,(Q\beta_j + \mathrm{base\_B0})\) |
| \(\frac{d\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dd}\) | \(\frac{\psi'\,d - \psi}{2d^2}\) |
| \(\frac{d^2\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dd^2}\) | \(\frac{\psi''\,d^2 - 2(\psi' d - \psi)}{2d^3}\) |
| \(\frac{d\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dt}\) (\(t = 1/d\)) | \(\frac{\psi - \psi'\,d}{2}\) |
| \(\frac{d^2\,\mathrm{UB2}}{dt^2}\) | \(\frac{\psi''}{2\,d^3}\) |
